title: Witch Week - Chapter 9 (Fragment 5)
text:
Nan talked, to take her mind off it. “How is this,” she said, “for romantic dreams come true? I always thought of myself flying a broomstick on a warm summer night, outlined against an enormous moon, with a nightingale or so singing its head off. And look at us!” Underneath her, the broomstick jerked. It was obviously a shrug.

“Yes, I daresay it is the best we can do,” Nan said. “But I don’t feel very glamorous like this, and I’m getting wet. I bet Dulcinea Wilkes used to sit on her broom, gracefully, sidesaddle probably, with her long hair flowing out behind. And because it was London, she probably wore an elegant silk dress, with lots of lacy petticoats showing from underneath. Did you know I was descended from Dulcinea Wilkes?”

The rippling underneath her might have been the broom’s way of nodding. But it could have been laughing at the contrast.

Nan found she could see in the dark now. She looked down and blenched. The broomstick felt very flimsy to be this high. It had soared and turned while she was talking, so that the square shapes of the school were a long way below and to one side. The pale spread of the playing field was directly underneath, and beyond that Nan could see the entire town, filling the valley ahead. The houses were all dark, with orange chains of streetlights in between. And in spite of the drifting drizzle, Nan could see as far as the blackness of Larwood Forest on the hill opposite.

“Let’s fly over the forest,” she said.

The broom swept off. Once you got used to it, it really would be a nice feeling, Nan told herself firmly, blinking against the drizzle. Secret, silent flight. It was in her blood. She held the end of the broom handle in both hands and tried to point it at the town. But the broom had other ideas. It wanted to go around the edge of the town. The result was that they flew sideways, jolting a little.

“Fly over the houses,” said Nan.

The broom gave a shake that nearly sent her tumbling off. No.

“I suppose someone might look up and see us,” Nan agreed. “All right. You win again. Bully.” And it occurred to her that her dreams of flying against a huge full moon were really the most arrant romantic nonsense. No witch in her senses would do that, for fear the inquisitors might see her.

So they swooped over fields, and across the main road in a rush of rain. The rain at first came at Nan’s face in separate drops out of the orange haze made by the streetlights. Then it was just wetness out of darkness, as they reached Larwood Forest, and the wetness brought a smell with it of autumn leaves and mushroom. But even a dark wood is not quite black at night. Nan could see paler trees, which still had yellow leaves, and she could clearly see mist caused by the rain smoking out above the trees. Some of it seemed to be real smoke. Nan smelled fire distinctly. A wet fire, burning smokily.

She suddenly felt rather quiet. “I say, that can’t be a bonfire, can it? If it’s after midnight, it is Halloween, isn’t it?”

The idea seemed to upset the broom. It stopped with a jerk. For a second, its front end tipped downwards as if it were thinking of landing. Nan had to grip hard in order not to slide off head first. Then it began actually flying backwards, wagging its brush in agitated sweeps, so that Nan’s feet were swirled from side to side.

“Stop it!” she said. “I shall be sick in a minute!”

They did, she knew, sometimes burn witch’s brooms with the witch they belonged to. So she was not surprised when the broom swung around, away from the smell of smoke, and began flying back towards the school, in a stately sort of way, as if that was the way it had meant to go all along.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or appearing troubled or nervous.
vocabulary.example: Then it began actually flying backwards, wagging its brush in agitated sweeps, so that Nan’s feet were swirled from side to side.

vocabulary.definition: Complete and utter; notorious.
vocabulary.example: ” And it occurred to her that her dreams of flying against a huge full moon were really the most arrant romantic nonsense.

vocabulary.definition: A large open-air fire used for celebrations or burning unwanted materials.
vocabulary.example: “I say, that can’t be a bonfire, can it?

vocabulary.definition: To be related to someone from earlier generations.
vocabulary.example: Did you know I was descended from Dulcinea Wilkes?

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is clear and easy to perceive.
vocabulary.example: Nan smelled fire distinctly.

vocabulary.definition: When hopes or wishes are fulfilled.
vocabulary.example: “How is this,” she said, “for romantic dreams come true?

vocabulary.definition: Being carried slowly by wind or water.
vocabulary.example: And in spite of the drifting drizzle, Nan could see as far as the blackness of Larwood Forest on the hill opposite.

vocabulary.definition: Light rain falling in very fine drops.
vocabulary.example: And in spite of the drifting drizzle, Nan could see as far as the blackness of Larwood Forest on the hill opposite.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely large in size or amount.
vocabulary.example: I always thought of myself flying a broomstick on a warm summer night, outlined against an enormous moon, with a nightingale or so singing its head off.

vocabulary.definition: Weak and insubstantial; easily damaged.
vocabulary.example: The broomstick felt very flimsy to be this high.

vocabulary.definition: Having an attractive or exciting quality that makes certain people or things seem appealing.
vocabulary.example: “But I don’t feel very glamorous like this, and I’m getting wet.

vocabulary.definition: In a smooth and elegant way.
vocabulary.example: I bet Dulcinea Wilkes used to sit on her broom, gracefully, sidesaddle probably, with her long hair flowing out behind.

vocabulary.definition: A slight obscuration of the lower atmosphere, typically caused by fine suspended particles.
vocabulary.example: The rain at first came at Nan’s face in separate drops out of the orange haze made by the streetlights.

vocabulary.definition: Officials who question people closely, especially for religious or political reasons.
vocabulary.example: No witch in her senses would do that, for fear the inquisitors might see her.

vocabulary.definition: To move with a quick, sudden motion.
vocabulary.example: ” Underneath her, the broomstick jerked.

vocabulary.definition: To shake or move abruptly.
vocabulary.example: The result was that they flew sideways, jolting a little.

vocabulary.definition: A small bird known for its beautiful song at night.
vocabulary.example: I always thought of myself flying a broomstick on a warm summer night, outlined against an enormous moon, with a nightingale or so singing its head off.

vocabulary.definition: To show the outer edge or shape of something.
vocabulary.example: I always thought of myself flying a broomstick on a warm summer night, outlined against an enormous moon, with a nightingale or so singing its head off.

vocabulary.definition: Having less color or brightness than usual.
vocabulary.example: Nan could see paler trees, which still had yellow leaves, and she could clearly see mist caused by the rain smoking out above the trees.

vocabulary.definition: Moving in small waves or undulations.

vocabulary.definition: Concerning love or idealized emotions.
vocabulary.example: “How is this,” she said, “for romantic dreams come true?

vocabulary.definition: A way of riding a horse with both legs on one side.
vocabulary.example: I bet Dulcinea Wilkes used to sit on her broom, gracefully, sidesaddle probably, with her long hair flowing out behind.

vocabulary.definition: To fly or rise high in the air.
vocabulary.example: It had soared and turned while she was talking, so that the square shapes of the school were a long way below and to one side.

vocabulary.definition: Having a dignified, unhurried, and grand manner.
vocabulary.example: So she was not surprised when the broom swung around, away from the smell of smoke, and began flying back towards the school, in a stately sort of way, as if that was the way it had meant to go all along.

vocabulary.definition: Wide, usually quick movements.

vocabulary.definition: To move rapidly downwards through the air.
